business_info: Patient thrombosis status encoded using categorical labels. K, M, Q, and Z represent different recorded states related to thrombosis assessment.

Leg-related symptoms in the Symptoms field may be documented using highly heterogeneous free text such as patient quotes, shorthand (e.g. "L leg pn"), or multi-symptom lists; downstream analyses may therefore choose between strict literal matching and broader text normalization depending on study goals.

In the Examination table, autoantibody measurements may be reported as either quantitative titers, qualitative flags, or hybrid text strings such as ">320" or "borderline"; analytical pipelines must decide whether to threshold on raw values or to map these textual encodings into binary elevated / not elevated status.

For out‑patient follow‑up, some visits are logged as "ambulatory" or "day care" in admission-related flags, which may or may not be grouped with standard outpatient clinic visits depending on the quality metric being computed.

Clinic staff sometimes capture leg complaints under generic descriptors like "mobility reduced" or "difficulty walking" rather than explicit anatomical terms, meaning that leg involvement can be under-ascertained if only direct leg keywords are searched.

Because of different assay generations, some antiphospholipid antibodies in Examination have lot-dependent normal ranges; internal projects therefore often define "elevated" relative to the 99th percentile of a reference group rather than a fixed cut point shared across antibodies.

Symptoms entries can include both physician-observed signs and patient-reported complaints in a single free-text string, without any enforced structure or controlled vocabulary separating leg-related content from other organ systems.

The term "outpatient clinic" in historical audit reports was sometimes used interchangeably with "polyclinic" and "external consult" even though downstream datasets might retain only one of these wordings in the admission flags.

In some analytic cohorts based on this database, outpatient follow-up is inferred from a combination of admission flags, absence of overnight stays in the HIS layer, and the presence of a scheduled follow-up date within 90 days, rather than from a single explicit "outpatient" marker.

Some retrospective studies using this dataset have grouped together different antiphospholipid antibody classes (IgG, IgM, IgA) into a single composite variable such as "any aPL positive" when modeling thrombosis risk, but this is not a requirement imposed by the raw schema.

In routine documentation, calf pain, thigh pain, and groin discomfort might all be variously described as "leg pain" or kept distinct, depending on the clinician; some service lines prefer to reserve the literal phrase "pain in leg" for below-knee symptoms only.

The Examination.Symptoms field occasionally contains negated expressions such as "no leg pain" or "denies swelling"; NLP experiments on this corpus have tried both including and excluding such strings when looking for leg-related symptoms.

Some research teams post-process the thrombosis outcome codes into a three-level ordinal scale (none / possible / confirmed) to simplify modeling, but the raw Examination.Thrombosis column itself does not enforce this grouping.

Within the thrombosis coding framework, higher alphabetical codes generally indicate increasing concern or severity, but local conventions may have evolved over time as new imaging methods became available.

The admission-related fields in Patient were first introduced during the migration from paper to electronic records, and were intended to distinguish referral source, current stay type, and planned discharge destination, but many legacy records have overlapping or inconsistent encodings.

Laboratory coagulation parameters like PT and APTT are not directly used to set the thrombosis label; instead, thrombosis coding reflects imaging-confirmed events, clinician gestalt, and documented clinical diagnoses abstracted from radiology and discharge notes.

In the early years of data capture, thrombosis codes were sometimes left blank for clearly negative cases under the assumption that absence of a code implied no thrombosis, whereas later on explicit "no thrombosis" labels were encouraged; this temporal drift can affect naive prevalence estimates.

Outpatient follow-up visits for thrombosis risk are often scheduled with hematology rather than general medicine; in some institutional dashboards, only hematology-led visits are counted as "thrombosis clinic" even if other specialties also see the patient.

In autoantibody interpretation, some labs distinguish between "low-titer" positives (just above normal) and "high-titer" positives (e.g. >40 GPL/MPL), and only the latter are treated as clinically relevant in risk scores, although the raw database may store both without this distinction.

The antibody_1, antibody_2, and antibody_3 columns in Examination were introduced at different times during the study; therefore, absence of a value in one column does not necessarily mean the test was not clinically indicated, just that it was not part of the protocol at that time.

When integrating Laboratory and Examination data, analysts sometimes treat Examination dates as index dates and allow a symmetric ±3 day window for lab results, assuming that coagulation and antibody tests performed within this window reflect the same clinical assessment episode.

Due to the tertiary referral nature of the center, many patients already have a history of thrombosis prior to the first record in the database; the Thrombosis field reflects status around the time of the examination, not necessarily lifetime history.

For quality improvement reports, outpatient clinic follow-up is sometimes defined as any patient with at least one non-emergency visit within 6 months of index admission, regardless of whether the specific clinic was hematology, internal medicine, or a subspecialty service.

Symptoms documentation is known to vary by specialty: hematology notes tend to emphasize bleeding or thrombosis-related signs, whereas rheumatology notes may prioritize systemic features such as rash or arthralgia even if leg symptoms are present but considered non-specific.

A substantial subset of Symptoms entries originates from templated clinic forms that pre-populate common complaints like "fatigue" or "joint pain"; clinicians then append free-text clarifications such as laterality or location, which might appear after a semicolon or in parentheses.

Free-text Diagnosis in the Examination table can include terms such as "DVT suspected" or "rule out PE" that are conceptually related to thrombosis but do not directly set the Thrombosis outcome code; diagnosis text and outcome codes are maintained as separate abstractions.

Some legacy data imports for this cohort used localized language descriptions for symptoms (e.g. non-English terms for "leg swelling"), which were later partially normalized; analysts should be aware that searching only English keywords will underestimate symptom counts.

When reporting percentages of patients with thrombosis, internal analytics usually define the denominator at the patient level (unique ID) but allow the numerator to reflect either at-least-one event or first event only, depending on the dashboard; SQL queries therefore often need a DISTINCT ID layer on top of examination-level filters.
